title: Tensor Runtime Architecture
use_for: mapping neural data into explicit 3D tensor layouts
defining tensor arena ownership, slices, precision, and lifetime
separating CPU-side shape/view tensors from runtime GPU-compatible tensors
planning command-stream friendly tensor memory contracts
do_not_use_for: using off-the-shelf ML frameworks as the default answer
generic GPU rendering without neural tensor ownership
rules: treat data architecture as the primary neural design surface
define tensor shape semantics before layer composition
map high-rank or semantic tensors into explicit 3D runtime slices
keep CPU-side views and runtime slices separate unless a single owner is deliberately chosen
make arena ownership, precision, offset, and lifetime explicit
avoid hidden copies, implicit reshapes, and framework-owned tensor lifetimes
procedure: Name the tensor roles and semantic axes.
Choose the runtime 3D projection for each tensor.
Define contiguous layout, offset ownership, precision, and resize policy.
Define which tensors are CPU-side views, runtime slices, parameters, activations, gradients, or scratch.
Define command compatibility: offsets, lengths, shape fields, broadcast rules, and dispatch dimensions.
Review batch-size changes, checkpointing, and diagnostics against the same tensor contracts.
output: runtime tensor and command-stream compatibility plan
CPU/GPU data boundary and telemetry plan

primary_when: the request is about neural tensor layout, tensor arena, runtime data architecture, or 3D tensor representation
the request is about making neural data command-stream or GPU-backend compatible
the request treats data ownership and memory layout as more important than model layer selection
must_not_own_when: the request is mainly about UI, mod loader behavior, assembly parsing, or non-neural GPU rendering

solves: how to design neural data as explicit tensor memory instead of framework-managed objects
how to preserve semantic axes while executing on compact 3D runtime slices
how to make tensor allocation, resizing, precision, upload, download, and command fields reviewable
how to prevent training and inference code from hiding costly CPU/GPU data movement
does_not_solve: choosing a pretrained model family by popularity
delegating tensor lifetime to PyTorch, TensorFlow, Barracuda, or similar frameworks
GPU rendering pipelines that do not own neural tensor data

core_principles: The tensor layout is the neural runtime architecture.
A 3D runtime tensor should encode the execution shape: batch, sequence/space, and feature/channel where possible.
High-rank CPU-side tensor views are acceptable at boundaries, but hot runtime execution needs explicit contiguous slices.
Arena slices should expose offset, length, dimensions, chunk count, and precision without hidden ownership.
Commands should carry enough shape and offset data to run without reconstructing object graphs.
GPU residency is an ownership decision, not a backend detail.
Telemetry should measure arena use, transfer volume, dispatch count, and readback cost.
constraints: Do not introduce a third-party ML framework as the foundation for core tensor runtime decisions.
Do not let layer code allocate unmanaged temporary tensors without an arena or lifetime contract.
Do not make shape transforms implicit if they change contiguity, axis meaning, or command compatibility.
Do not download full arena or gradient state in hot paths unless the operation is explicitly diagnostic or checkpointing.
Do not mix CPU view tensors and GPU runtime tensors without documenting the synchronization point.
Do not hide precision conversion behind generic upload or download calls.
decision_rules: Use tensor-runtime-architecture as primary when the request mentions tensor layout, arena, GPU tensor, 3D tensor, batch/sequence/feature layout, or runtime data ownership.
Attach architecture when layer composition, forward graph, attention, embeddings, or model topology is also in scope.
Attach training when batches, targets, losses, gradients, optimizers, or checkpoints are in scope.
Attach optimization when throughput, numerical stability, memory movement, precision, or GPU residency is in scope.
Attach gpu support when tensor slices are executed by compute kernels or command streams.
Attach performance memory arena support when allocation, pooling, chunking, or fragmentation is the main risk.

preferred_patterns: two-layer tensor model: semantic CPU tensor views and explicit runtime tensor slices
3D projection table for batch/sequence/feature, batch/classes/sequence, or x/y/z volume tensors
arena-owned offsets with explicit chunk, precision, and resize policy
command structs that reference tensor offsets and dimensions instead of object graphs
separate tensors for parameters, activations, gradients, accumulators, optimizer state, and scratch
dirty range or residency state for selective upload, download, and checkpointing
avoid_patterns: layer objects owning hidden arrays outside the arena
shape-only abstractions that do not define memory order
implicit flattening that loses batch or sequence semantics
runtime dispatch that depends on reconstructing high-level model objects
diagnostic gradient downloads enabled by default in throughput tests

recipes.name: Define a 3D neural tensor contract
recipes.when: Use before implementing a neural layer, dataset handoff, loss, or GPU command path.
steps: Name each semantic axis and decide how it maps into SizeX, SizeY, and SizeZ.
Record the flat memory order and contiguous requirement.
Record the arena owner, slice lifetime, precision, and resize policy.
Record whether the tensor is parameter, activation, gradient, optimizer state, scratch, input, target, or diagnostic.
Record which commands or kernels may read and write the slice.

recipes.name: Separate semantic tensor views from runtime slices
recipes.when: Use when dataset, tokenizer, or transformer code needs rich shape operations but backend execution requires compact slices.
steps: Keep CPU-side view tensors responsible for rank, strides, reshape, transpose, and validation.
Convert to runtime slices at explicit ingestion, compile, or upload boundaries.
Reject non-contiguous views unless a materialization step is explicitly scheduled.
Store the resulting 3D runtime dimensions with the slice.
Make synchronization explicit before backend execution or checkpointing.

recipes.name: Review GPU residency readiness
recipes.when: Use when a CPU-backed tensor arena becomes a bottleneck for GPU training or inference.
steps: Identify the current source of truth for each tensor group.
Classify uploads and downloads as input ingestion, diagnostics, checkpointing, loss readback, or accidental sync.
Add dirty range or residency state before changing backend behavior.
Move hot activations, parameters, gradients, and optimizer state to GPU-resident ownership first.
Keep explicit download paths for checkpoints, validation, and bounded telemetry.

pitfalls: Treating 3D tensor dimensions as cosmetic metadata instead of dispatch and memory contracts.
Flattening sequence data into one dimension and then rebuilding semantics inside every layer.
Letting CPU-side `Tensor` and runtime `GpuTensor` drift into two unrelated data models.
Optimizing kernels before eliminating full-arena transfers.
Allowing diagnostic gradient norm checks to become default training cost.
Adding precision fields without defining where conversion and storage ownership happen.
review_checklist: Is every hot tensor mapped to an explicit 3D runtime shape?
Can each slice's owner, precision, lifetime, and resize policy be named?
Are CPU view tensors and runtime slices synchronized only at explicit boundaries?
Can command structs execute from offsets and dimensions without object graph lookups?
Are parameters, activations, gradients, accumulators, optimizer state, and scratch separated?
Are upload/download boundaries justified and measured?
Does batch-size change preserve tensor contracts without hidden allocation churn?
Does telemetry expose arena use, transfer volume, dispatch count, and readback cost?

references.title: Neural Tensor Runtime Architecture
references.interpretation: Use this first for 3D tensor layout, arena ownership, semantic/runtime tensor split, and command compatibility.
references.why: It captures the domain's primary design lens.

references.title: Neural GPU Residency
references.interpretation: Use when tensor data movement, GPU source-of-truth, dirty ranges, or readback policy is in scope.
references.why: It connects neural tensor layout to GPU throughput bottlenecks.

references.title: Neural Training Runtime
references.interpretation: Use when the tensor runtime is exercised through batches, losses, gradients, and optimizer state.
references.why: Training validates whether the tensor architecture survives real update flows.
